---
title: "Tutorial 11 — Jupyter Magic"
jupyter: python3
---

tikzpics ships an IPython extension that lets you write raw TikZ code directly in
a notebook cell with the `%%tikz` magic — no Python API required. This is useful
for quick sketches or for pasting existing TikZ snippets.

## Loading the extension

Load the extension once per session with `%load_ext`:

```{python}
#| eval: false
%load_ext tikzpics
```

## Basic usage

After loading the extension, any cell starting with `%%tikz` is compiled as a
`tikzpicture` environment and the resulting image is displayed inline:

```{python}
#| eval: false
%%tikz
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[shape=circle, color=white, fill=blue] (n1) at (0, 0) {A};
    \node[shape=circle, color=white, fill=blue] (n2) at (5, 0) {B};
    \draw[->, line width=2, color=gray] (n1) to (n2);
\end{tikzpicture}
```

## Passing options

Options after `%%tikz` are forwarded to `TikzFigure` — for example, to set
the output scale:

```{python}
#| eval: false
%%tikz --scale 2
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \draw[thick, fill=orange!40] (0,0) rectangle (2,1);
    \node at (1, 0.5) {Hello};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

## When to use the magic vs. the Python API

| | `%%tikz` magic | Python API |
|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Existing TikZ snippets, quick sketches | Programmatic / data-driven figures |
| **Requires** | IPython / Jupyter | Any Python environment |
| **Output** | Inline image | Inline image, PDF, PNG |
